sectionName: Psychological, Social & Biological Foundations of Behavior

questions.passage: A sociologist examined educational mobility in 600 young adults. Participants were grouped by highest parental education: high school or less, some college, or bachelor's degree or higher. Researchers recorded the percentage of participants who completed a bachelor's degree by age 25 and the percentage who reported an unpaid internship during college. Degree completion rates were 18%, 37%, and 68% across the three parental-education groups. Internship rates were 9%, 24%, and 49%, respectively. Interviews suggested that family financial buffers, familiarity with campus norms, and access to professional contacts shaped who could take low-paid or unpaid opportunities that later improved résumés.
questions.stem: Which parental-education group had the highest rate of completing a bachelor’s degree by age 25?

choices.B: Participants whose parents had a bachelor’s degree or higher
choices.A: Participants whose parents had some college
choices.C: Participants whose parents had high school or less
choices.D: All groups completed at similar rates

explanations.B: Correct. The figure shows a clear gradient: 68% of participants with BA+-educated parents completed a bachelor’s degree, compared with 37% and 18% in the two lower parental-education groups.
explanations.A: The some-college group had an intermediate rate, not the highest.
explanations.C: The high-school-or-less group had the lowest completion rate in the figure.
explanations.D: The groups differ substantially, so equal completion is inconsistent with the data.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Degree completion and internship participation rates by parental education level.

questions.stem: The pattern in the passage most directly illustrates which concept?

choices.D: Social reproduction through unequal access to institutional resources

explanations.D: Correct. Children from more advantaged educational backgrounds had greater access to internships and degree completion, suggesting that institutions help reproduce existing status advantages. The passage emphasizes unequal resources and cultural familiarity, both central to social reproduction.
explanations.A: The looking-glass self concerns self-concept formation through imagined judgments of others.
explanations.B: Fundamental attribution error is a bias in explaining behavior, not a structural account of educational inequality.
explanations.C: Gate-control theory explains pain perception, not educational mobility.

questions.stem: Which finding from Figure 1 most supports the interview claim about professional contacts?

choices.A: Internship participation rose sharply as parental education increased
choices.B: Degree completion was below 20% in the lowest parental-education group
choices.C: All three groups reported the same number of siblings
choices.D: Participants from all backgrounds said college was important

explanations.A: Correct. Unpaid internships often depend on networks and financial support. The strong increase in internship participation from 9% to 49% across parental-education groups fits the idea that families with more institutional familiarity and contacts help students access résumé-building opportunities.
explanations.B: Low completion rates show disadvantage, but they do not specifically connect to access to professional contacts.
explanations.C: Sibling counts do not address the mechanism proposed in the interviews.
explanations.D: Valuing college is not the same as having access to internships or networks.

questions.stem: Which policy would most directly address the inequality highlighted by the internship data?

choices.C: Providing paid internship stipends for students with financial need
choices.A: Requiring students to declare a major by the first semester
choices.B: Extending library hours during finals week
choices.D: Asking employers to advertise only to graduating seniors

explanations.C: Correct. If unpaid internships are easier to accept when families can absorb the lost income, paid stipends would reduce that barrier. This policy targets the resource inequality that the passage links to internship access and later résumé advantages.
explanations.A: Declaring a major earlier does not directly reduce the financial barrier to unpaid work experience.
explanations.B: Longer library hours may help academically, but they do not specifically address unequal access to internships.
explanations.D: Restricting recruitment to seniors does not solve the structural resource gap described in the passage.

questions.stem: Why would the study be limited in making strong causal claims about parental education?

choices.B: Participants were grouped by an existing background characteristic rather than randomly assigned to parental-education categories
choices.A: Researchers measured both internships and degree completion
choices.C: The sample size was larger than 500

explanations.B: Correct. Parental education is an observed social characteristic, not an experimentally manipulated variable. Because families differ on many related factors, the results show strong associations but cannot isolate parental education as the sole cause of the outcomes.
explanations.A: Measuring more than one outcome does not by itself weaken causal inference.
explanations.C: A large sample improves precision, but it does not solve confounding by background variables.
explanations.D: Adding interviews can enrich interpretation; it does not automatically block causal claims.

questions.passage: A city health department surveyed adolescents in three neighborhoods that differed in concentrated disadvantage. Neighborhood 1 had the lowest poverty rate, Neighborhood 2 was intermediate, and Neighborhood 3 had the highest poverty and vacancy rates. Researchers measured perceived neighborhood cohesion on a 1–10 scale and depressive-symptom scores on a 0–30 scale. Mean cohesion scores were 7.8, 6.1, and 4.3; depressive-symptom scores were 8.4, 11.6, and 15.2. Youth in the highest-disadvantage neighborhood also reported the longest average commute to school and the lowest access to supervised after-school programs.
questions.stem: As neighborhood disadvantage increased, Figure 2 shows what overall pattern?

choices.A: Cohesion decreased while depressive symptoms increased
choices.B: Both cohesion and depressive symptoms increased
choices.C: Both cohesion and depressive symptoms decreased
choices.D: Depressive symptoms were unchanged while cohesion decreased

explanations.A: Correct. Moving from Neighborhood 1 to Neighborhood 3, cohesion falls from 7.8 to 4.3, while depressive-symptom scores rise from 8.4 to 15.2. The figure therefore shows an inverse pattern between cohesion and distress across neighborhoods.
explanations.B: The figure does not show both variables increasing together.
explanations.C: Depressive symptoms rise rather than fall as disadvantage increases.
explanations.D: Depressive symptoms change substantially across neighborhoods, so they are not unchanged.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 2. Mean neighborhood cohesion and depressive-symptom scores across three neighborhoods differing in concentrated disadvantage.

questions.stem: The passage most strongly supports which sociological interpretation?

choices.D: Place-based conditions are associated with mental-health differences
choices.A: Adolescent mood is determined entirely by genetics
choices.B: After-school programs are unrelated to distress
choices.C: Students choose longer commutes because they prefer stress

explanations.D: Correct. The study links neighborhood disadvantage, weaker cohesion, and higher depressive symptoms. Although the design is observational, it supports the broader idea that social and environmental context is associated with mental-health outcomes.
explanations.A: Nothing in the study isolates genetics as the sole determinant of mood.
explanations.B: The passage actually notes lower access to after-school programs in the highest-disadvantage neighborhood, so dismissing them is not supported.
explanations.C: The commute pattern is presented as part of structural context, not a simple preference for stress.

questions.stem: Which additional observation would best fit the concept of social capital?

choices.C: Youth who know more trusted adults in their neighborhood report lower distress
choices.A: Youth who have larger shoe sizes report lower distress
choices.B: Neighborhood 3 residents watch more comedy videos online
choices.D: Adolescents in all neighborhoods have identical ages

explanations.C: Correct. Social capital refers to resources embedded in social ties and trust. Knowing more trusted adults would provide support, information, and monitoring, making it a direct indicator of the kind of community resource implied by cohesion.
explanations.A: Shoe size is unrelated to neighborhood-based social resources.
explanations.B: Video habits may affect mood, but they are not themselves measures of neighborhood social capital.
explanations.D: Equal age across neighborhoods would not directly capture the trust and ties central to social capital.

questions.stem: A city planner hoping to improve the pattern in Neighborhood 3 should most directly target which factor?

choices.B: Access to supervised after-school programs and community spaces
choices.A: The number of planets visible at night
choices.C: The biological sex ratio at birth
choices.D: Whether adolescents prefer pop or country music

explanations.B: Correct. Because the most disadvantaged neighborhood had low cohesion and fewer supervised programs, expanding safe spaces and structured programs could plausibly strengthen social ties and reduce stress exposure. That intervention directly addresses the contextual factors mentioned in the passage.
explanations.A: Night sky visibility is not part of the mechanism discussed in the study.
explanations.C: Sex ratio at birth is unrelated to the neighborhood-level factors described here.
explanations.D: Music preference does not address structural disadvantage or cohesion.

questions.stem: What is the main methodological reason the data do not prove that low cohesion causes depressive symptoms?

choices.D: The study compares existing neighborhoods, so unmeasured confounders may contribute to both variables
choices.A: The figure contains only three neighborhoods
choices.B: Cohesion was measured on a 10-point scale

explanations.D: Correct. Because youths were not randomly assigned to neighborhoods, many correlated factors—such as school quality, family income, or exposure to violence—could influence both cohesion and depressive symptoms. The study therefore supports association, not definitive causation.
explanations.A: Having three neighborhoods limits breadth but is not the key reason causation cannot be established.
explanations.B: Using a rating scale does not itself prevent causal inference.
explanations.C: Including commute information adds context; it does not explain the causal limitation.
